results.possibleIdentity: Fina Miralles (born Josefina Miralles Nobell in 1950) is a prominent Spanish (Catalan) conceptual artist who was particularly active starting in the early 1970s. Her work is associated with the Catalan Conceptual group and involves a multidisciplinary approach including Land Art, performance, photography, and documentation. She is noted for exploring the relationship between the natural and the artificial, as seen in her 1973 work 'Translacions. Dona-arbre' (Translations. Woman-tree). Her name, Josephina Miralles, appears in historical archives such as the Morris/Trasov Archive at the Belkin Art Gallery, which documents the 'Eternal Network' of artists and includes items related to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Her practice during the 1970s frequently intersected with the experimental and conceptual trends that E.A.T. promoted, focusing on new ways of documenting and mediating artistic experience.

Josefina Aguilar (1945–2026) is a world-renowned Mexican folk artist from Ocotlán de Morelos, Oaxaca, known primarily by her first name. She became an international historical figure in the 1970s when her signature red clay 'muñecas' (dolls) were 'discovered' and collected by Nelson Rockefeller. Though her work is traditional and rooted in Mexican folklore—depicting religious figures, market scenes, and historical characters—her rise to fame in the 1970s and her status as a 'one-name' artist make her a possible identity for 'Josephina'. However, she is not typically associated with the high-technology collaborations characteristic of the E.A.T. group.
